summary: Hannes Mühleisen explains the technical foundations of DuckDB, an in-process analytical database built on vectorized query execution and morsel-driven parallelism. He describes how DuckDB originated from academic research at CWI Amsterdam, driven by the observation that no one had designed a data management system prioritizing usability and ease of data import/export. The conversation covers how vectorized execution processes data in 2,048-row column-oriented chunks to stay in CPU cache, and how morsel-driven parallelism improves on older exchange-based approaches by making operators parallelism-aware and reactive. Hannes argues that most data problems don't need distributed systems since hardware scales faster than human-generated data, and criticizes academia for solving 'whale problems' instead of helping the 99% of practitioners stuck on tools like Pandas.
key_points: DuckDB uses vectorized query execution with 2,048-row column-oriented chunks to keep data in CPU cache, achieving ~100x speedup over constant memory round-trips
Morsel-driven parallelism replaces exchange-based parallelism by making operators parallelism-aware, solving skew and resource fairness problems
The in-process embedded model (like SQLite) was chosen over client-server to maximize usability, but requires rethinking crash handling, resource sharing, and system design
Hardware scales faster than data because human data production is bounded by population, making distributed systems unnecessary for 99% of use cases
DuckDB bridges academia and industry by applying state-of-the-art database research (from CWI Amsterdam and TU Munich) in production-quality software with real engineering practices
money_quotes: We realized that nobody had really considered the ergonomics of using data management systems. It was always very complicated to set up.

We combine this crazy academic sort of knowledge on how data systems should be built, all wrapped into a package that tries to be friendly. And that's something that I don't think has been done before.
Hardware scales faster than data sets because the humans scale slower than hardware. We have a natural limit on monkeys hitting keyboards.
It's absolutely idiotic to start throwing distributed systems at 99% of data problems.
As an academic, it's actually quite criminal to solve the whales' problems because the whales don't care. They have clever people. They will ignore solutions that we have come up with anyway.
By controlling the size of the intermediates, we can make the query execution stay in the CPU cache for longer, and ideally forever.
The quality of software produced in academia is typically very bad because it's just a means to an end.
We were sick and tired of the quality of research prototypes. We said, we'll do it for real this time.
key_insight: By starting from usability first principles rather than traditional database architecture, DuckDB arrived at an in-process, vectorized, morsel-parallel design that brings state-of-the-art academic database research to the 99% of data practitioners underserved by existing tools.
